summary:
The Norte Chico civilisation (also known as the Caral-Supe civilisation), flourishing approximately 3000–2000 BC along the coastal valleys of north-central Peru, is the earliest known complex society in the Americas and one of the six independent cradles of civilisation in human history. The civilisation comprised at least 30 major sites in the Supe, Pativilca, Fortaleza, and Huaura river valleys, with the large site of Caral serving as a prominent centre. Its existence challenges earlier assumptions about the prerequisites for civilisational complexity in the New World.

Norte Chico society was built on a distinctive economic foundation: unlike Mesopotamian and Egyptian civilisations, which were based primarily on cereal agriculture, Norte Chico subsistence combined coastal fishing and anchovy harvesting with inland agriculture focused not on food crops but primarily on cotton and gourds. The cotton was used to produce fishing nets and textiles, while gourds served as net floats — suggesting an economy centred on the production of fishing technology rather than food. This maritime-agricultural symbiosis supported large populations and sustained the labour needed for monumental construction.

The monumental architecture of Norte Chico is extraordinary for its age. The sites contain large platform mounds constructed from stone-filled bags (shicra) and sunken circular plazas, used for public ceremonial activities. The largest platform mound at Caral is comparable in volume to the Egyptian pyramids of the same era. The civilisation also left evidence of quipus — knotted string devices that later Inca civilisation used for record-keeping — raising the possibility that Norte Chico had developed an early information-storage system.

Norte Chico is remarkable for what it lacked as much as for what it possessed: there is little evidence of warfare, weapons, or defensive fortifications in the archaeological record, suggesting that this early civilisation organised large populations through religious authority and economic interdependence rather than military coercion. The civilisation declined around 2000 BC, possibly due to climate change and disruptions to the maritime-agricultural exchange system, but it established a template for Andean civilisation that would be inherited and developed by later cultures.
